paper_title: Vitamin C and the Common Cold: A Meta-Analysis
tldr: Across randomized trials, routine vitamin C supplementation does not reduce cold incidence in the general population, but it modestly shortens illness duration; benefits are larger in cohorts under heavy physical stress (e.g., marathon runners, soldiers) and when taken continuously as prophylaxis rather than initiated after symptom onset.

highlights: We investigate whether vitamin C prevents common cold incidence and reduces illness duration.
The objective is to quantify prophylactic versus therapeutic effects via randomized trials.
Despite widespread use, consensus on vitamin C's effect on the common cold remains mixed.
Prior reviews conflated incidence and duration outcomes, obscuring subgroup effects.
We conduct a random-effects meta-analysis of double-blind, placebo-controlled RCTs.
Studies are stratified by prophylaxis vs. treatment and by high-physical-stress cohorts.
We separate incidence and duration endpoints and provide subgroup estimates with heterogeneity.
We release a cleaned dataset of trial effect sizes and code for reproducibility.
No significant reduction in incidence for general population prophylaxis.
Duration reduced by ~8% overall; ~14–20% in heavy-exertion cohorts.

notes.metrics: Risk ratio for incidence; standardized percent reduction for duration.
notes.heterogeneity: Moderate between-study heterogeneity for duration outcomes.
notes.bias: Small-study effects detected; trim-and-fill does not eliminate main patterns.
notes.limitations: Therapeutic initiation post-symptom onset shows limited effect; dosing regimens vary.
